name: Standup facilitator suite
description: Five agents that coach the FACILITATOR of a daily standup in real time. Demonstrates queue capture, a silent fact writer, per-speaker memory via $inc, Phase-1 -> Phase-2 event coordination (action item -> owner/date confirmation), a silence-triggered timebox nudge, and cooldown gating on rapid-fire action items.

agents.name: Action Item Detector
agents.text: Detect when someone commits to a task or is assigned one, and capture it.
agents._doc: Phase-1 capturer. On task-commitment language it pushes the item onto the `action_items` queue AND emits the `action_item_logged` EVENT that wakes the Assignment Confirmer in Phase 2 of the SAME turn. Cooldown is 12s on purpose: when two action items land <12s apart, the second is GATED by cooldown (watch the 'skipped: cooldown' line) -- so its queue push AND its event are both lost, which is exactly why the Confirmer goes quiet that turn. This is the lesson: a cooldown on a Phase-1 emitter silently suppresses the Phase-2 hop too.

speak.content: Captured an action item. Make sure it has a named owner and a due date before you move on.

agents.name: Assignment Confirmer
agents.text: When an action item is captured, prompt the facilitator to lock down owner and deadline.
agents._doc: EVENT-triggered. Runs ONLY in Phase 2, and ONLY on turns where Action Item Detector emitted `action_item_logged`. On every other turn it appears under 'skipped (trigger_type_mismatch)'. Its rule gates on `when.has_event` -- a live blackboard read of the event the Phase-1 agent just emitted. This is the clearest view of one-hop coordination and the +1 LLM round-trip (and added latency) it costs. When the detector is cooldown-gated, no event is emitted, so this agent stays silent that turn even though an action item was clearly stated -- a deliberate teaching moment.

speak.content: That's a real commitment. Confirm out loud who owns it and when it's due, then it's tracked.

agents.name: Decision Tracker
agents.text: Silently record any decision the team reaches.
agents._doc: Silent fact writer (no whisper). On decision language ('we decided', 'let's go with', 'agreed', 'final call') it records a `decision` fact on the Blackboard. Watch the Facts section of the Blackboard panel -- it never speaks, it only writes structured state other agents and the host can read.

facts.value: team reached a decision in standup

agents.name: Participation Tracker
agents.text: Track how much each teammate has spoken and flag when the floor is unbalanced.
agents._doc: Per-speaker memory via `$inc`. Each rule is gated on `when.speaker` (the last segment's speaker) and increments a counter in this agent's PRIVATE memory -- watch the Memory section of the Blackboard panel: sam_turns and priya_turns climb independently. Because mock rules cannot read a memory counter inside `when`, the imbalance WARNING is gated by `min_turn` (fires once the floor has demonstrably tilted toward Sam) rather than by reading the counter. The lesson: per-speaker memory accumulates cleanly across turns, but a mock rule can only branch on what's in the prompt or on the blackboard (events/vars/facts/queues) -- not on its own memory values.

speak.content: Sam has driven most of this standup. Explicitly invite Priya and Dev to add anything before you wrap.

agents.name: Timebox Monitor
agents.text: Watch for the meeting stalling and nudge the facilitator to keep it moving.
agents._doc: SILENCE-triggered. It subscribes ONLY to the silence trigger, so it stays out of every turn_based turn (you will see it under 'skipped: trigger_type_mismatch' on speaking turns). Its rule has an EMPTY `when`, so the moment the host reports dead air it speaks -- that is the canonical silence-agent shape. The matching scenario step uses "trigger":"silence" with a silence_duration; that is the host's job (the engine never invents silence). Compare with the Sales demo, where NO silence agent existed and the silence turn fired nothing: trigger types only ever wake agents that subscribe to them.

speak.content: The room has gone quiet and you're over time. Park the open thread, take it offline, and close the standup.
